behaviors.description: The traveling-salesman heuristic produces a route that is no more than 1.5x the length of the optimal route for inputs up to 20 stops.
behaviors.justification: This is primarily an algorithm-quality property of the heuristic itself (input coordinates -> route length ratio). No OS/network/subprocess/LLM dependency is required. I considered Integration, but the behavior text does not require cross-module handoff; it only asserts heuristic performance bound.
behaviors.test_description: Property-style unit test over many coordinate sets (n<=20): compute optimal length via exact solver, compute heuristic length, assert heuristic/optimal <= 1.5.

behaviors.description: The delivery time window estimates are computed correctly from the distance between consecutive stops and the configured average speed for the road type.
behaviors.justification: This behavior depends on module wiring: consecutive-leg distances + road-type speed configuration + estimator output. Unit tests on formulas can pass while integration fails due to config lookup or leg metadata handoff. No real OS/network/subprocess is needed, and no AI output is involved.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test through service-layer estimator path with a small multi-leg route and real config objects/files used in-process; assert each stop window matches expected time accumulation by road-type speed.

behaviors.description: The shift-hours validator rejects a route whose total estimated time exceeds the driver's remaining shift hours, returning the excess amount.
behaviors.justification: This is a deterministic validator rule on two numeric inputs (estimated total vs remaining shift) plus excess calculation. No additional cross-boundary reality signal is needed beyond unit tests.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test on validator function: provide total time and remaining shift where total>remaining; assert rejected status and exact excess value.

behaviors.description: When one address in the list fails geocoding (API returns no results), the service skips that stop and optimizes the remaining addresses, reporting which addresses were skipped.
behaviors.justification: Failure handling requires interaction across geocoding result handling, stop filtering, optimizer input, and skipped-address reporting. Unit-only tests can miss handoff/state bugs. I considered System because geocoding is an external API, but the behavior text is about internal handling once a 'no results' outcome is produced, not verifying real HTTP contract/round-trip side effects.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test with an in-process geocoding stub/adaptor returning coordinates for most addresses and 'no results' for one; assert optimized route excludes failed stop and response includes that address in skipped list.

behaviors.description: Duplicate addresses in the input list are consolidated into a single stop with combined delivery items, not visited twice.
behaviors.justification: This depends on orchestration between input normalization/deduplication, item aggregation, and route construction. Unit tests of dedupe logic alone may miss integration defects where optimizer still receives duplicates or aggregated items are dropped.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test through request-processing pipeline: submit duplicated address entries with distinct items; assert output route has one stop for that address and combined items attached once.
